name: Field of Formal Generalized Power Series (Laurent Series)
name_norm: field of formal generalized power series (laurent series
statement: For a field k, k((x)) is the set of formal sums ∑_{n=n0}^∞ a_n x^n with n0 ∈ Z and a_n ∈ k. Addition is termwise and multiplication is convolution. The set k((x)) is a field with these operations.

name: Lemma 8.9: Number of F-homomorphisms Equals Separable Degree
name_norm: number of f-homomorphisms equals separable degree
statement: Let K be a finite extension of F, and let S be the separable closure of F in K. Then [S : F] is equal to the number of F-homomorphisms from K to an algebraic closure of F.
proof_sketch: Embed K in an algebraic closure M. By the primitive element theorem, S = F(α) for some α, with r = [S : F]. The minimal polynomial of α over F has r distinct roots α₁,…,αᵣ in M. The maps σᵢ: S → M defined by f(α) ↦ f(αᵢ) are exactly the r distinct F-homomorphisms from S to M. Since K/S is purely inseparable, K is normal over S, so each σᵢ extends to an F-homomorphism K → M.

name: Corollary 9.7: Intermediate Fields of Cyclic Kummer Extensions
name_norm: intermediate fields of cyclic kummer extensions
statement: Let K/F be a cyclic extension of degree n, and suppose F contains a primitive n-th root of unity. If K = F(n√a) with a ∈ F, then every intermediate field of K/F is of the form F(m√a) for some divisor m of n.
proof_sketch: Let σ generate Gal(K/F). Subgroups are ⟨σ^t⟩ for t|n; write n=tm. Then σ^t fixes a^m=(n√a)^m, so F(a^m) ⊆ Fix(σ^t). By Prop 9.6, order of a^m F* is m, so [F(a^m):F]=m. The Fundamental Theorem gives [Fix(σ^t):F]=m, thus they are equal.

name: Purely transcendental field extension
statement: A field K is purely transcendental over a subfield F if K is isomorphic to a field of rational functions over F in some number, finite or infinite, of variables. If K = F(t_1, ..., t_n) with {t_1, ..., t_n} algebraically independent, then K is often said to be a rational extension of F.

name: Radical Extensions are n-Radical
statement: If K = F(a1, ..., ar) is a radical extension with integers n1, ..., nr as in the definition, then K is an n-radical extension of F for n = n1 ··· nr.
proof_sketch: Since a_i ∈ F(a1, ..., a_{i-1}) and n is a multiple of each n_i, we have a_i^n ∈ F(a1, ..., a_{i-1}) for all i, satisfying the condition for an n-radical extension with uniform n.

name: Chain Characterization of Radical Extensions
statement: K is a radical extension of F if and only if there is a chain of fields F = F0 ⊆ F1 ⊆ ... ⊆ Fr = K such that F_{i+1} = F_i(a_i) for some a_i ∈ F_{i+1} with a_i^{n_i} ∈ F_i for some integer n_i.
proof_sketch: Forward direction: Set F_i = F(a1,...,a_i). Then F_{i+1} = F_i(a_{i+1}) with a_{i+1}^{n_{i+1}} ∈ F_i. Converse: If a chain exists, then a_i ∈ F_i, so K = F(a1,...,ar) and the n_i satisfy the radical extension condition.

name: Transitivity of Radical Extensions
statement: If K/F is a radical extension and L/K is a radical extension, then L/F is a radical extension.
proof_sketch: Let K = F(a1,...,ar) with a_i^{n_i} ∈ F(a1,...,a_{i-1}) and L = K(b1,...,bs) with b_j^{m_j} ∈ K(b1,...,b_{j-1}). Adjoining all a_i then all b_j gives a chain from F to L satisfying the radical condition. The combined chain yields a radical extension from F to L.

name: Characteristic of an Integral Domain
statement: If R is an integral domain, then its characteristic char(R) is either 0 or a prime number.
proof_sketch: Consider the map φ: Z → R sending n to n·1. Its image is a subring, and Z/ker(φ) is an integral domain, so ker(φ) is a prime ideal. Since ker(φ) is generated by char(R), char(R) must be 0 or prime.

name: Prime implies Irreducible
statement: If a is a prime element in an integral domain R, then a is irreducible.
proof_sketch: Suppose a = bc. Then a divides bc. Since a is prime, a divides b or a divides c. If a|b, then b = ad, so a = adc, implying dc = 1, so c is a unit. Similarly, if a|c, then b is a unit. Hence a is irreducible.

name: Irreducibles are Prime in a UFD
name_norm: irreducibles are prime in a ufd
statement: If R is a UFD and π ∈ R is irreducible, then π is prime.
proof_sketch: Suppose π divides ab, so ab = πc. Factor c into irreducibles: c = θ₁...θ_k. Then πθ₁...θ_k is a factorization of ab into irreducibles. By unique factorization, the irreducible π must appear (up to associates) in the factorization of a or b. Thus π divides a or π divides b.

name: Determination of F-automorphisms by generators
statement: Let K = F(X) be a field extension of F generated by a subset X of K. If σ, τ ∈ Gal(K/F) satisfy σ|_X = τ|_X, then σ = τ. Thus, F-automorphisms of K are uniquely determined by their action on a generating set.
proof_sketch: Any a ∈ K can be written as a rational function f(x_1,…,x_n)/g(x_1,…,x_n) with x_i ∈ X and coefficients in F. Since σ, τ fix F and respect addition/multiplication, applying them to this expression yields the same result if they agree on X.

name: Real square roots and odd degree polynomials have roots
name_norm: real square roots and odd degree polynomials have roots
statement: 1. If f(x) = x^2 - a for some a > 0, then f has a root in ℝ. Therefore, every nonnegative real number has a real square root. 2. If deg(f) is odd, then f has a root in ℝ. Consequently, the only odd degree extension of ℝ is ℝ itself.
proof_sketch: For part 1, f(0) < 0 and f(u) > 0 for large u; by the Intermediate Value Theorem there is c ∈ [0,u] with f(c) = 0. For part 2, assuming positive leading coefficient, limits as x→∞ and x→-∞ go to ∞ and -∞; the Intermediate Value Theorem gives a real root. If L/ℝ is an odd degree extension, take a ∈ L∖ℝ; then [ℝ(a):ℝ] is odd, so the minimal polynomial of a has odd degree and a real root, forcing it to be linear, so a ∈ ℝ and L = ℝ.

name: Properties of closed sets
statement: In a topological space X, the closed sets satisfy: (1) X and ∅ are closed; (2) if A and B are closed, then A ∪ B is closed; (3) if {A_i} is a collection of closed sets, then ⋂_i A_i is closed.
proof_sketch: Follows immediately from the definition of a topology and DeMorgan's laws: complement of union is intersection of complements, and complement of intersection is union of complements.

name: Standard topology on ℝ
statement: A nonempty subset U ⊆ ℝ is open if for every x ∈ U there exists δ > 0 such that (x - δ, x + δ) ⊆ U. Together with ∅, this defines the standard topology on ℝ.
proof_sketch: Easy exercise to verify the topology axioms.

name: Metric space topology
statement: Let (X, d) be a metric space. A nonempty subset U ⊆ X is open if for every x ∈ U there exists δ > 0 such that the open ball B(x, δ) = {y ∈ X : d(x, y) < δ} is contained in U. This defines the metric space topology.
proof_sketch: Straightforward verification using metric space axioms.

name: Finite complement topology
statement: On a set X, the finite complement topology is defined by declaring a proper subset of X closed if it is finite. Equivalently, a nonempty subset is open if its complement is finite.
proof_sketch: Verification: finite unions and arbitrary intersections of finite sets are finite, so closed sets satisfy axioms.

name: Finite-Dimensional Vector Space
statement: If V has a finite basis, then V is said to be a finite dimensional vector space.
proof_sketch: Definition.

name: Theorem 19.14: Existence and Extension of Transcendence Bases
name_norm: existence and extension of transcendence bases
statement: Let K be a field extension of F. (1) There exists a transcendence basis for K/F. (2) If T ⊆ K such that K/F(T) is algebraic, then T contains a transcendence basis for K/F. (3) If S ⊆ K is algebraically independent over F, then S is contained in a transcendence basis of K/F. (4) If S ⊆ T ⊆ K, with S algebraically independent over F and K/F(T) algebraic, then there is a transcendence basis X for K/F with S ⊆ X ⊆ T.
proof_sketch: Statement (4) implies the others by suitable choices of S and T. To prove (4), consider the set of all algebraically independent subsets of T containing S, ordered by inclusion. By Zorn’s lemma it has a maximal element M. If K were not algebraic over F(M), then F(T) would not be algebraic over F(M), so some t∈T is transcendental over F(M), and by Lemma 19.13, M∪{t} is algebraically independent, contradicting maximality. Hence K is algebraic over F(M), making M a transcendence basis within the desired bounds.

name: Chain-complete subset collections have maximal elements
name_norm: chain-complete subset collections have maximal elements
statement: Let X be a set and let S be a nonempty collection of subsets of X, partially ordered by inclusion. If for every chain T in S the union ∪T is an element of S, then S has a maximal element.
proof_sketch: For any chain T, ∪T is an upper bound for T (since each t ∈ T satisfies t ⊆ ∪T) and ∪T ∈ S by hypothesis. Zorn's Lemma then implies S has a maximal element.

name: Chain-complete partial function collections have maximal elements
name_norm: chain-complete partial function collections have maximal elements
statement: Let X,Y be sets and let S be a nonempty collection of pairs (A,f) where A ⊆ X and f: A → Y, ordered by (A,f) ≤ (B,g) iff A ⊆ B and g|A = f. If for every chain T in S the pair (∪T, h) with h defined by h(x) = g(x) whenever (X,g) ∈ T and x ∈ X is an element of S, then S has a maximal element.
proof_sketch: For a chain T, (M, h) with M = ∪T is an upper bound in S by construction (the function h is well-defined because T is a chain). By hypothesis (M,h) ∈ S, so Zorn's Lemma applies.

name: Independence of fiber restriction map for locally compact spaces
name_norm: independence of fiber restriction map for locally compact spaces
statement: Let X be a locally compact Hausdorff space and T a compact connected Hausdorff space. Then the induced homomorphism i_t^* : H_c^*(X × T; F × T) → H_c^*(X; F) is independent of the choice of t ∈ T.
proof_sketch: For any cohomology class a, define K(a) = {t ∈ T : a ∈ Ker i_t^*}. Using 10.6, K(a) is open. Given t and β = i_t^*(a), the class a − π^*β lies in Ker i_t^*, so t ∈ K(a − π^*β). Consequently, for s near t, i_s^*(a) = i_s^*(π^*β) = β, showing s ↦ i_s^*(a) is locally constant. Since T is connected, the value is constant.

name: Leray Sheaves for Vector Bundles
statement: Let ξ be an n-plane bundle over a space Y with total space U. Identify Y with the zero section, set U₀ = U - Y, and let π: U → Y, π₀ = π|U₀. Then the Leray sheaf J^q(π; L) is the constant sheaf with stalks L for q = 0 and 0 otherwise; J^q(π, π₀; L) is the locally constant sheaf with stalks H^q(ℝ^n, ℝ^n - {0}; L).
proof_sketch: Using an admissible local trivialisation V × ℝ^n, homotopy invariance reduces the computation to the disk relative to its boundary. Then 4.2 and 7.1 imply the constant and locally constant nature of the sheaves, with the claimed stalks.

name: Transfer isomorphism for sheaves of L-modules (prime characteristic)
name_norm: transfer isomorphism for sheaves of l-modules (prime characteristic
statement: Let ℱ be a sheaf of L-modules, where L is a field whose characteristic is relatively prime to the order of G. Then the map π*: H_Φ^*(X/G; ℱ) → H_Φ^*(X; π*ℱ)^G is an isomorphism.
proof_sketch: From the relations μ* π*(b) = (ord G) b and π* μ*(a) = Σ g*(a), the image of π* lies in the G-invariants. Since ord G is invertible in L, multiplication by ord G is an isomorphism, hence π* is injective and its image is exactly the invariant subgroup, yielding the isomorphism.

name: Finite group action preserves cohomological local connectedness over a field of prime characteristic
name_norm: finite group action preserves cohomological local connectedness over a field of prime characteristic
statement: If X is a Hausdorff space and n-clc_L (cohomologically locally connected of degree n over a field L) with char(L) relatively prime to ord G, then the orbit space X/G is also n-clc_L.
proof_sketch: For n=0 the statement is clear. For n>0, use the transfer to relate cohomology of neighborhoods in X and X/G. Given a point in X/G and an open neighborhood, lift to X; the G-action and invertibility of |G| in L allow one to average cocycles, showing that the relevant cohomological conditions descend from X to X/G (details in the surrounding proof sketch).

name: Stable Quotient of an Ideal
statement: The stable quotient of I with respect to f, denoted I : f^∞, is the ideal {g ∈ R : there exists n ≥ 1 for which f^n g ∈ I}.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Nakayama's Lemma Corollary (Submodule Equality)
statement: Let Q be a local ring with maximal ideal m, let M be a finitely generated Q-module, and let N be a submodule of M. If M = mM + N, then M = N.
proof_sketch: Observe that m(M/N) = (mM + N)/N. The condition M = mM + N gives m(M/N) = M/N. Applying Nakayama's Lemma to M/N yields M/N = 0, hence M = N.

name: Adapted Monomial Order
statement: A monomial order on k[z1,...,zm,w1,...,wn] is said to be adapted to a given linear function ℓ (presumably tailored for integer programming problems). (The full definition is truncated in the excerpt.)

name: Local convergence of fixed point iteration
name_norm: local convergence of fixed point iteration
statement: If g is differentiable at a fixed point z (g(z) = z) and |g'(z)| < 1, then the iteration z_{k+1} = g(z_k) converges to z for all starting values z_0 sufficiently close to z. Moreover, the smaller |g'(z)| is, the faster the convergence; the case g'(z) = 0 is especially favorable.
proof_sketch: A special case of the Contraction Mapping Theorem; the proof shows that near z the map g is a contraction with constant |g'(z)|+ε < 1.

name: Gröbner Basis Algorithm for Hilbert Bases
name_norm: gröbner basis algorithm for hilbert bases
statement: Let G be a Gröbner basis for I with respect to any elimination order > for which all z_i, t > v_j, and all v_j > w_k. Let S be the subset of G consisting of binomials v^\alpha - w^\alpha for \alpha \in Z_{\ge 0}^N. Then H = \{ \alpha : v^\alpha - w^\alpha \in S \} is the Hilbert basis for K.
proof_sketch: Idea similar to Theorem (1.11); see [Stu1] for full exposition.

name: Pure n-dimensional Polyhedral Complex
statement: A polyhedral complex ∆ ⊂ R^n is said to be pure n-dimensional if every maximal element of ∆ (with respect to inclusion) is an n-dimensional polyhedron.

name: Adjacent Polytopes in a Complex
statement: Two n-dimensional polytopes in a complex ∆ are said to be adjacent (definition continues in text).

name: Graded Resolution
statement: A graded resolution of a graded R-module M is a resolution ... → F_2 → F_1 → F_0 → M → 0 where each F_i is a graded free module and each differential is a graded homomorphism of degree zero.

name: Supporting Hyperplane of a Polytope
statement: A supporting hyperplane H of a convex polytope P ⊂ ℝⁿ is a hyperplane such that P is entirely contained in one of the closed half-spaces determined by H and H ∩ P is non-empty.
proof_sketch: This is a definition; no proof is required.

name: Radical and Radical Ideal
statement: Let I ⊂ k[x1, ..., xn] be an ideal. The radical of I is √I = {g ∈ k[x1, ..., xn] : g^m ∈ I for some integer m ≥ 1}. I is a radical ideal if √I = I.
proof_sketch: N/A

name: Radical Ideal Property
statement: For every ideal I ⊂ k[x1, ..., xn], √I is an ideal containing I.
proof_sketch: See [CLO], Chapter 4, §2.

name: Minimal Element of a Submodule
statement: Let M be a nonzero submodule of k[x]^2. A minimal element of M with respect to a monomial order > is an element g ∈ M \ {0} such that LT(g) is minimal with respect to >.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Order Domain
statement: A pair (R, ρ) is an order domain if ρ is an order function on R.

name: Identity Value of Order Function
statement: ρ(1) = 0, and ρ(f) = 0 if and only if f is a nonzero constant in Fq.
proof_sketch: By the product axiom ρ(1) = ρ(1·1) = ρ(1)+ρ(1) forces ρ(1)=0. If ρ(f)=0, write f in a basis with distinct ρ-values; using linear independence and the bound on ρ of sums forces f to be constant.

name: Linear Independence of Distinct ρ Values
name_norm: linear independence of distinct ρ values
statement: Any set of elements of R with distinct ρ values is linearly independent over Fq.
proof_sketch: Assume a nontrivial linear combination sums to zero. Take the term with maximal ρ-value; by the properties ρ(c·f)=ρ(f) for c≠0 and ρ(f+g) ≤ max(ρ(f),ρ(g)), the maximal ρ-value cannot cancel, contradiction.

name: Definition of a Lattice Polytope
statement: A lattice polytope is a set of the form Conv(A), where A ⊂ ℤⁿ is finite. These are convex hulls of sets of points with integer coordinates.

name: Definition of an Affine Hyperplane
statement: An affine hyperplane in ℝⁿ is defined by an equation of the form m·ν = −a, where ν is a nonzero vector in ℝⁿ and a is a real number.

name: Definition of Supporting Hyperplane and Inward Normal
name_norm: definition of supporting hyperplane and inward normal
statement: For a polytope Q ⊂ ℝⁿ and a nonzero vector ν ∈ ℝⁿ, let a_Q(ν) = −min_{m∈Q} (m·ν). The equation m·ν = −a_Q(ν) is called a supporting hyperplane of Q, and ν is called an inward pointing normal.

name: Uniqueness of Products up to Isomorphism
name_norm: uniqueness of products up to isomorphism
statement: If A and B are both products of the same family {A_i}, then A and B are isomorphic.
proof_sketch: By the universal property of A, there is a unique θ: B → A making the product diagrams commute. Symmetrically, there is a unique η: A → B. Then θ η and η θ are morphisms into the products themselves; by uniqueness of the mediating morphism for the identity maps, θ η = id_A and η θ = id_B, so they are isomorphisms.
depends_on: product of a family of objects

name: Ker-Coker Exact Sequence
statement: Given a commutative diagram with exact columns, set K_i = ker φ_i and L_i = coker φ_i. Then the induced sequence K_1 → K_2 → K_3 → L_1 → L_2 → L_3 is exact.
proof_sketch: Form a double complex with the given diagram and zero elsewhere; rows are complexes and columns are short exact. The homology at B_j is K_j and at C_j is L_j. Applying the long exact homology sequence (Theorem 3.3) yields the exact sequence connecting them.

name: Construction of local coordinate charts on Y(Γ)
name_norm: construction of local coordinate charts on y(γ
statement: For a congruence subgroup Γ of SL2(Z) and any point π(τ) ∈ Y(Γ), let U be a neighborhood as in Corollary 2.2.3, set h = |Γτ|, and define δ(z) = (z - τ)/(z - τ̅) (a map taking τ to 0 and τ̅ to ∞). Then ψ(z) = (δ(z))^h identifies points of U exactly under the Γ-action, inducing a homeomorphism ϕ: π(U) → ψ(U) ⊂ C that serves as a local coordinate chart on the modular curve Y(Γ).
proof_sketch: By Proposition 2.2.2 the stabilizer Γτ is cyclic of order h, and δ conjugates it to rotations z ↦ e^{2πi d/h} z. Corollary 2.2.3 guarantees that any identification in U comes from Γτ. Hence π(z1) = π(z2) ⇔ δ(z1) = e^{2πi d/h} δ(z2) ⇔ ψ(z1) = ψ(z2). The map ψ is open by the Open Mapping Theorem, yielding a bijective continuous open map ϕ defined by ϕ(π(z)) = ψ(z), which is therefore a homeomorphism.

name: Connectedness of X(Γ)
statement: The extended upper half-plane H* is connected, and therefore its continuous image X(Γ) is connected.
proof_sketch: Assume H* = O1 ∪ O2 with disjoint open sets. Intersecting with connected H forces one to contain H, so the other is contained in Q ∪ {∞}. But any nonempty subset of Q ∪ {∞} cannot be open in H*; thus one is empty, proving H* connected. X(Γ) is a continuous image, hence connected.

name: Dirichlet L-function
statement: For a Dirichlet character χ modulo N and Re(s) > 1, L(s, χ) = ∑_{n=1}^∞ χ(n) n^{-s} = ∏_{p prime} (1 - χ(p) p^{-s})^{-1}.

name: Hilbert Basis Theorem (Special Case for Polynomial Rings)
name_norm: hilbert basis theorem (special case for polynomial rings
statement: The ring k[x1, ..., xn] is Noetherian, meaning that all ideals are finitely generated, or equivalently there is no infinite strictly increasing chain I1 ⊂ I2 ⊂ I3 ⊂ · · · of ideals.
proof_sketch: Standard proof, e.g., by induction on n; see Fulton [Ful69].

name: Relation of Orders of Vanishing under Norm
name_norm: relation of orders of vanishing under norm
statement: For any nonzero f ∈ C(X)^*, ν_y(norm_h f) = ∑_{x ∈ h^{-1}(y)} ν_x(f).
proof_sketch: Exercise 6.2.2(b).

name: Cosets Partition a Group
statement: Let H be a subgroup of a group G. The left cosets of H form a partition of G; the right cosets of H form a partition of G.
proof_sketch: Define relation xRy iff xy^{-1} ∈ H. R is reflexive (xx^{-1}=1∈H), symmetric (xy^{-1}∈H ⇒ yx^{-1}=(xy^{-1})^{-1}∈H), transitive (xy^{-1}, yz^{-1}∈H ⇒ xz^{-1}=(xy^{-1})(yz^{-1})∈H). Thus R is an equivalence relation. xRy iff x ∈ Hy, so equivalence classes are right cosets. Left cosets are obtained similarly from xLy iff y^{-1}x ∈ H.
